title: 2.1.2 — Terminal lifecycle and panic hook
notes:
## ratatui 0.26 API note

In ratatui 0.26.3, the `Frame` struct has `fn size(&self) -> Rect`, NOT `fn area()`. The `area()` method was introduced in a later version. Use `frame.size()` everywhere.

The placeholder `run()` function should use:
```rust
let area = frame.size();  // NOT frame.area()
```

## Ctrl-C handling

crossterm in raw mode intercepts Ctrl-C. It arrives as a `KeyEvent` with `KeyCode::Char('c')` and `KeyModifiers::CONTROL`. The placeholder `run()` function should handle it:

```rust
if matches!(key.code, KeyCode::Char('q') | KeyCode::Esc)
    || (key.code == KeyCode::Char('c') && key.modifiers.contains(KeyModifiers::CONTROL))
{
    break;
}
```

Without this, Ctrl-C won't work and the user will be stuck in raw mode.

## `player` parameter usage

The `run()` placeholder function accepts `player` and `args` parameters but doesn't use them yet. Prefix with underscore to avoid clippy warnings: `_player: Player, _args: &Args`.

title: 2.1.3 — App struct and skeleton event loop
3.0 — Action enum and input module (Phase 3 foundation)
description:
## Summary

Create `input.rs` with a full `Action` enum and key-event-to-action mapping function. Refactor the inline `handle_key` in `app.rs` to use action-based dispatch. This is the foundation ticket for Phase 3 — all three parallel epics (controls bar, navigation/speed, help overlay) build on this.

## Context

Key handling is currently inline in `app.rs::handle_key()` (lines 89-117) with a direct `match key.code` that handles only Space, q/Esc, Ctrl+c, and arrow keys. There is no `input.rs` module and no `Action` enum. Phase 3 adds ~15 new keybindings across three parallel epics. Without a shared Action enum merged first, all three epics would create conflicting structural changes to `app.rs`.

## Files

- `crates/speedrun/src/input.rs` — NEW file
- `crates/speedrun/src/app.rs` — refactor `handle_key`, add `handle_action` dispatcher
- `crates/speedrun/src/main.rs` — add `mod input;`

## Tasks

1. **Create `input.rs` with the `Action` enum.** Define all variants needed for Phase 3:
   - `Quit`
   - `TogglePlayback`
   - `SeekForward` (5s)
   - `SeekBackward` (5s)
   - `SeekForward30s`
   - `SeekBackward30s`
   - `StepForward`
   - `StepBackward`
   - `SpeedUp`
   - `SpeedDown`
   - `NextMarker`
   - `PrevMarker`
   - `JumpToPercent(u8)` (0-9 → 0%-90%)
   - `JumpToStart`
   - `JumpToEnd`
   - `ToggleControls`
   - `ToggleHelp`

2. **Implement `map_key_event(KeyEvent) -> Option<Action>`.** Map all keybindings from the spec:
   - `q` → Quit
   - `Esc` → Quit (dispatch layer will intercept when help is visible — that is NOT this ticket's concern)
   - `Ctrl+c` → Quit
   - `Space` → TogglePlayback
   - `Right` → SeekForward
   - `Left` → SeekBackward
   - `Shift+Right` → SeekForward30s
   - `Shift+Left` → SeekBackward30s
   - `.` → StepForward
   - `,` → StepBackward
   - `+` or `=` → SpeedUp
   - `-` → SpeedDown
   - `]` → NextMarker
   - `[` → PrevMarker
   - `0`-`9` → JumpToPercent(digit)
   - `Home` or `g` → JumpToStart
   - `End` or `G` (Shift+g) → JumpToEnd
   - `Tab` → ToggleControls
   - `?` (Shift+/) → ToggleHelp
   - All other keys → None (ignored)

   Note: filter `key.kind != KeyEventKind::Press` before calling this function (keep that in the caller).

3. **Refactor `app.rs`.** Split `handle_key` into two methods:
   - `handle_key(&mut self, key: KeyEvent)` — filters non-Press events, updates `last_interaction`, calls `map_key_event`, dispatches to `handle_action`.
   - `handle_action(&mut self, action: Action)` — match on Action. Implement existing actions (Quit, TogglePlayback, SeekForward, SeekBackward). All new actions get no-op arms with `// TODO: implemented in Phase 3 epic` comments for now.

4. **Wire module.** Add `pub mod input;` to `main.rs` (matching existing `pub mod ui;` pattern). Import `Action` and `map_key_event` in `app.rs`.

5. **Write unit tests in `input.rs`.** Test every key mapping:
   - Each keybinding produces the correct Action
   - Modifier combos work (Shift+Right → SeekForward30s, not SeekForward)
   - Unmapped keys (e.g., `x`, `F1`) return None
   - Digit keys 0-9 map to correct JumpToPercent values
   - `G` (Shift+g) → JumpToEnd, `g` (no shift) → JumpToStart

## Important: ratatui/crossterm version notes

This project uses crossterm 0.27. Key events include `kind` field — only `KeyEventKind::Press` should be handled (release events are filtered). Shift+letter keys: crossterm reports `KeyCode::Char('G')` with `KeyModifiers::SHIFT` for Shift+g.

**KeyEvent construction in tests:**
```rust
use crossterm::event::{KeyCode, KeyEvent, KeyModifiers};

// Simple key (Press event by default):
KeyEvent::new(KeyCode::Char('q'), KeyModifiers::NONE)

// With modifiers:
KeyEvent::new(KeyCode::Right, KeyModifiers::SHIFT)  // Shift+Right
KeyEvent::new(KeyCode::Char('c'), KeyModifiers::CONTROL)  // Ctrl+c
KeyEvent::new(KeyCode::Char('G'), KeyModifiers::SHIFT)  // Shift+g (uppercase G)

// Special keys:
KeyEvent::new(KeyCode::Esc, KeyModifiers::NONE)
KeyEvent::new(KeyCode::Tab, KeyModifiers::NONE)
KeyEvent::new(KeyCode::Home, KeyModifiers::NONE)
KeyEvent::new(KeyCode::End, KeyModifiers::NONE)
```

Test pattern: call `map_key_event(key_event)` and assert on the returned `Option<Action>`:
```rust
assert_eq!(map_key_event(KeyEvent::new(KeyCode::Char('q'), KeyModifiers::NONE)), Some(Action::Quit));
assert_eq!(map_key_event(KeyEvent::new(KeyCode::Char('x'), KeyModifiers::NONE)), None);
```
Derive `Debug, PartialEq` on `Action` to enable `assert_eq!` comparisons.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `crates/speedrun/src/input.rs` exists with `Action` enum containing all 17 variants listed above
- [ ] `map_key_event` handles all keybindings from the spec and returns `Option<Action>`
- [ ] `app.rs` `handle_key` delegates to `map_key_event` → `handle_action`
- [ ] Existing behavior is preserved: Space toggles, q/Esc quits, arrows seek ±5s, Ctrl+c quits
- [ ] `last_interaction` is still updated on every keypress
- [ ] `show_controls = true` is still set on seek actions
- [ ] New actions (SpeedUp, StepForward, etc.) are matched but are no-ops for now
- [ ] Unit tests cover all key mappings including edge cases (Shift+arrows, digit keys, unmapped keys)
- [ ] `cargo build --workspace` passes with zero warnings
- [ ] `cargo test --workspace` passes
- [ ] `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` passes
- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes

title: Epic 1.5 — Player
description:
The main public API for speedrun-core. The Player owns the Recording, TimeMap, KeyframeIndex, and current Vt instance. It provides loading, seeking, continuous playback, and single-step navigation.

## Context

This is the capstone of the core engine — it integrates all previous modules into a single API that the TUI (and future WASM player) consumes. The spec defines the full public API surface in SPEC.md lines 169–248.

### Key design decisions

- **`PlayerError`** wraps `ParseError` and `TimeMapError` (the two fallible operations during loading).
- **Idle limit resolution** precedence: `LoadOptions.idle_limit` > header `idle_time_limit` > no limit.
- The Player owns a `Vt` instance that is **rebuilt on every seek** (restored from keyframe snapshot + event replay) and **mutated in-place during tick/step**.
- **`screen()` returns `&[avt::Line]`** (via `vt.view()`). The spec says `&avt::Screen` but avt does not export a `Screen` type; `&[Line]` is the actual API.
- **Marker times** are converted from raw to effective at load time.
- **`step_backward()`** is internally a seek (avt is forward-only, so we must replay from a keyframe).
- **Auto-pause at end**: `tick()` returns `true` once for the final frame, `false` on subsequent calls.

### avt API surface (verified)

- `Vt::view() -> &[Line]` — line grid for rendering
- `Vt::cursor() -> Cursor` — cursor with `col`, `row`, `visible` fields
- `Vt::size() -> (usize, usize)` — (cols, rows)
- `Vt::feed_str(&mut self, s: &str) -> (Vec<usize>, bool)` — feed data, returns dirty lines (ignored)
- `Vt::dump() -> String` / `TerminalSnapshot::capture/restore` — state serialization (used by keyframe index)

## Scope

- `crates/speedrun-core/src/player.rs` (new)
- Updates to `lib.rs`

## Tickets

- 1.5.1 — Player struct, load, seek, and screen access
- 1.5.2 — Playback control, tick, and stepping
- 1.5.3 — Player tests

title: 1.5.1 — Player struct, load, seek, and screen access
description:
Define the Player struct, error types, load pipeline, seek engine, and state accessors. This is the foundation that all playback control builds on.

## Tasks

1. Create `crates/speedrun-core/src/player.rs` with error type:

   ```rust
   #[derive(Debug)]
   pub enum PlayerError {
       Parse(ParseError),
       TimeMap(TimeMapError),
   }
   ```

   Implement `std::fmt::Display`, `std::error::Error`, `From<ParseError>`, and `From<TimeMapError>`.

2. Define `LoadOptions`:

   ```rust
   pub struct LoadOptions {
       /// Cap idle time between events (seconds).
       /// `Some(limit)` overrides the header's `idle_time_limit`.
       /// `None` means use the header value, or no limit if the header
       /// doesn't specify one either.
       pub idle_limit: Option<f64>,
   }

   impl Default for LoadOptions {
       fn default() -> Self { Self { idle_limit: None } }
   }
   ```

3. Define `Player` struct (fields are private):

   ```rust
   pub struct Player {
       recording: Recording,
       time_map: TimeMap,
       index: KeyframeIndex,
       vt: avt::Vt,
       /// Current position in effective time.
       current_time: f64,
       /// Index of the next event to process (into recording.events).
       current_event_index: usize,
       /// Markers with effective times (converted at load).
       markers: Vec<Marker>,
       /// Whether playback is active.
       playing: bool,
       /// Playback speed multiplier.
       speed: f64,
   }
   ```

4. Implement `Player::load(reader: impl Read) -> Result<Self, PlayerError>`:
   - Delegates to `load_with(reader, LoadOptions::default())`.

5. Implement `Player::load_with(reader: impl Read, opts: LoadOptions) -> Result<Self, PlayerError>`:
   - Parse recording: `parse(reader)?`
   - Resolve idle limit: `opts.idle_limit.or(recording.header.idle_time_limit)` — CLI override > header > None (no limit).
   - Extract raw timestamps: `recording.events.iter().map(|e| e.time).collect()`
   - Build time map: `TimeMap::build(&raw_times, resolved_limit)?`
   - Build keyframe index: `KeyframeIndex::build(&recording, &time_map)`
   - Create initial Vt: `create_vt(header.width as usize, header.height as usize)`
   - Convert markers to effective time: iterate `recording.events` by index, for each `EventType::Marker`, look up `time_map.effective_time(i)` and build a `Marker { time: effective_time, label }`.
   - Initialize state: `current_time = 0.0`, `current_event_index = 0`, `playing = false`, `speed = 1.0`.

6. Implement the internal replay method `fn replay_to(&mut self, target_time: f64)`:
   - Find nearest keyframe: `self.index.keyframe_at(target_time)`.
   - **If found**: get the keyframe, restore Vt via `keyframe.snapshot.restore()`, set `self.current_event_index = keyframe.event_index`.
   - **If not found** (target before first keyframe, or empty index): create a fresh Vt via `create_vt(header.width, header.height)`, set `self.current_event_index = 0`.
   - Replace `self.vt` with the restored/fresh Vt.
   - Feed events forward from `current_event_index`:
     ```rust
     while self.current_event_index < self.recording.events.len() {
         let Some(t) = self.time_map.effective_time(self.current_event_index) else { break };
         if t > target_time { break; }
         match &self.recording.events[self.current_event_index] {
             // Output: vt.feed_str(&data)
             // Resize: vt.feed_str(&format!("\x1b[8;{rows};{cols}t"))
             // Input/Marker: skip
         }
         self.current_event_index += 1;
     }
     ```
     Note: the condition is `t > target_time` (i.e., include events AT target_time). After the loop, `current_event_index` points to the first event NOT yet processed.
   - Set `self.current_time = target_time`.

7. Implement `seek(&mut self, time: f64)`:
   - Clamp `time` to `[0.0, self.duration()]`.
   - Call `self.replay_to(clamped_time)`.

8. Implement `seek_relative(&mut self, delta: f64)`:
   - `self.seek(self.current_time + delta)`.

9. Implement accessors:
   - `screen(&self) -> &[avt::Line]` — returns `self.vt.view()`
   - `cursor(&self) -> CursorState` — returns `CursorState::from_vt(&self.vt)`
   - `size(&self) -> (u16, u16)` — `let (c, r) = self.vt.size(); (c as u16, r as u16)`
   - `current_time(&self) -> f64`
   - `duration(&self) -> f64` — returns `self.time_map.duration()`
   - `markers(&self) -> &[Marker]` — returns `&self.markers`

10. Add `pub mod player;` to `lib.rs`. Re-export: `pub use player::{Player, PlayerError, LoadOptions};`

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `PlayerError` with `Display`/`Error`/`From` impls for both inner error types
- [ ] `LoadOptions` with `Default` impl
- [ ] `Player::load()` and `load_with()` successfully load all valid test data files
- [ ] Idle limit resolution: CLI override > header `idle_time_limit` > no limit
- [ ] `seek()` clamps to [0, duration] and restores correct terminal state
- [ ] `seek_relative()` works for positive and negative deltas
- [ ] `screen()` returns `&[avt::Line]` from the current Vt view
- [ ] `cursor()` returns correct `CursorState`
- [ ] `size()` returns current Vt dimensions as `(u16, u16)`
- [ ] `duration()`, `current_time()`, `markers()` return correct values
- [ ] Marker times are effective (not raw)
- [ ] Module added to `lib.rs` with re-exports
- [ ] `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` passes
- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes
notes:
## Codebase context

**avt API (verified from source):**
- `Vt::view() -> &[avt::Line]` — no `avt::Screen` type exists. Adapt the spec's `screen() -> &avt::Screen` accordingly.
- `Vt::size() -> (usize, usize)` — returns `(cols, rows)`, cast to `u16` for public API.
- `Vt::cursor() -> avt::Cursor` — has `.col`, `.row`, `.visible` fields. `CursorState::from_vt()` already extracts these.
- `Vt::feed_str() -> (Vec<usize>, bool)` — ignore the return value for playback.

**Resize parameter order**: `EventData::Resize { cols, rows }` but xtwinops is `\x1b[8;{rows};{cols}t`. Same pattern as in the keyframe indexer.

**Marker conversion**: `Recording.markers` has raw times. Iterate `recording.events` by index to find `EventType::Marker` events and look up `time_map.effective_time(event_index)` for each. The parser ensures markers appear in both `events` and `markers` vecs, but the events vec gives us the index for TimeMap lookup.

**`replay_to()` event inclusion**: Events with `effective_time <= target_time` are included (fed to Vt). After replay, `current_event_index` points to the first event NOT yet processed. This ensures `tick()` (next ticket) doesn't double-process events.

## Verification commands

```bash
cargo build -p speedrun-core
cargo clippy -p speedrun-core -- -D warnings
cargo fmt --check
```

title: 1.5.2 — Playback control, tick, and stepping
description:
Implement continuous playback advancement (tick), playback state control (play/pause/speed), frame timing for the event loop, and single-event stepping.

## Tasks

1. **Playback state control:**

   ```rust
   pub fn play(&mut self)          // sets playing = true
   pub fn pause(&mut self)         // sets playing = false
   pub fn toggle(&mut self)        // flips playing
   pub fn is_playing(&self) -> bool
   pub fn set_speed(&mut self, speed: f64)
   pub fn speed(&self) -> f64
   ```

2. **`tick(&mut self, dt: f64) -> bool`** — advance playback by `dt` wall-clock seconds:

   - If not playing, return `false` immediately.
   - Compute `new_time = self.current_time + dt * self.speed`.
   - Clamp `new_time` to `self.duration()`.
   - Track whether any events were processed (`state_changed = false`).
   - Feed events using the same loop pattern as `replay_to`:
     ```rust
     while self.current_event_index < self.recording.events.len() {
         let Some(t) = self.time_map.effective_time(self.current_event_index) else { break };
         if t > new_time { break; }
         // Output: feed to vt, state_changed = true
         // Resize: feed to vt, state_changed = true
         // Input/Marker: skip
         self.current_event_index += 1;
     }
     ```
   - Update `self.current_time = new_time`.
   - **Auto-pause at end**: if `new_time >= self.duration()`, set `self.playing = false` and `state_changed = true` (so the TUI re-renders the final frame with paused state).
   - Return `state_changed`.

3. **`time_to_next_event(&self) -> Option<std::time::Duration>`** — for the TUI event loop:

   - If not playing, return `None`.
   - If `current_event_index >= events.len()`, return `None` (at end).
   - Get the next event's effective time: `time_map.effective_time(current_event_index)`.
   - Compute wall-clock delta: `(next_effective_time - current_time) / speed`.
   - If delta <= 0 (event is overdue), return `Some(Duration::ZERO)`.
   - Convert positive delta to `Duration` and return `Some(duration)`.

4. **`step_forward(&mut self) -> bool`** — advance to the next output event:

   - If `self.playing`, return `false` (stepping only when paused).
   - Scan from `current_event_index` forward, looking for `EventType::Output`. Process each event as you scan:
     - `Resize`: feed to Vt (resizes affect Vt state and must not be skipped).
     - `Input` / `Marker`: skip (no Vt state change).
     - `Output`: feed to Vt, update `current_time` to this event's effective time, set `current_event_index` past it, return `true`.
   - If loop exhausts all events without finding an output event, return `false` (at end).

5. **`step_backward(&mut self) -> bool`** — seek to the previous output event:

   - If `self.playing`, return `false` (stepping only when paused).
   - **Guard against underflow**: if `current_event_index == 0`, return `false` (already at start, no previous event exists). Do NOT compute `current_event_index - 1` without this check — it panics on usize underflow.
   - Scan backward from `current_event_index - 1` toward 0, looking for `EventType::Output`.
   - If found: call `self.seek(effective_time_of_that_event)`. This rebuilds from a keyframe since avt is forward-only.
   - If no previous output event found, return `false`.
   - Return `true`.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `play()`/`pause()`/`toggle()`/`is_playing()` work correctly
- [ ] `set_speed()`/`speed()` work correctly
- [ ] `tick()` advances time by `dt × speed`, feeds events to Vt, returns whether state changed
- [ ] `tick()` returns `false` when not playing
- [ ] `tick()` auto-pauses at end of recording, returns `true` for the final frame
- [ ] Subsequent `tick()` calls after reaching end return `false`
- [ ] `time_to_next_event()` returns correct wall-clock `Duration` accounting for speed
- [ ] `time_to_next_event()` returns `None` when paused or at end
- [ ] `step_forward()` advances to next output event, processing intervening resizes
- [ ] `step_forward()` skips input/marker events
- [ ] `step_forward()` returns `false` at end of recording
- [ ] `step_backward()` seeks to previous output event via keyframe replay
- [ ] `step_backward()` returns `false` at start of recording (including when `current_event_index == 0`)
- [ ] Both step methods return `false` when `is_playing()` is true
- [ ] `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` passes
- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes
notes:
## Codebase context

**Depends on 1.5.1**: The `Player` struct, `replay_to()`, `seek()`, and all accessors already exist. This ticket adds playback advancement methods.

**`tick()` performance**: Called on every frame. The hot path processes only events in the `[current_time, new_time]` window — typically 1–3 events. `vt.feed_str()` handles these in microseconds.

**`step_backward()` cost**: Internally calls `seek()`, which restores from a keyframe and replays forward. Bounded by `KEYFRAME_INTERVAL` (5s of events) — still fast but more expensive than `step_forward()`.

**`time_to_next_event()` is the TUI's sleep duration**: The event loop calls `crossterm::event::poll(timeout)` with this value. Returning `None` means the loop uses a default idle poll interval (100ms). Returning `Duration::ZERO` means "there's an event ready right now."

**Event processing in `step_forward`**: Unlike `tick()` which skips non-output events silently, `step_forward` must process intervening resize events so the Vt dimensions stay correct. It steps through events one by one until it finds the next output event.

## Verification commands

```bash
cargo build -p speedrun-core
cargo clippy -p speedrun-core -- -D warnings
cargo fmt --check
```

title: 1.5.3 — Player tests
description:
Comprehensive test suite for all Player behavior: loading, seeking, playback, stepping, and metadata.

## Tasks

1. **Load tests:**
   - Smoke test: load all valid test data files in a loop (`minimal_v2.cast`, `minimal_v3.cast`, `empty.cast`, `long_idle.cast`, `with_markers.cast`, `with_resize.cast`, `alternate_buffer.cast`, `real_session.cast`) — verify no error and non-panic. This is one test function, not eight.
   - Idle limit resolution: load `long_idle.cast` with explicit `idle_limit` override via `LoadOptions` — verify duration differs from loading without override. Load without override — verify header's `idle_time_limit` is applied (duration should be shorter than raw).
   - Error cases: empty bytes and binary garbage — verify appropriate `PlayerError` variant.

2. **Seek tests** (including screen content verification):
   - `seek(0.0)` — verify cursor at origin, screen is blank (initial state).
   - `seek(known_event_time)` — load `minimal_v2.cast`, seek to the effective time of the last event, verify `screen()[0].text()` contains expected output.
   - `seek(between_events)` — verify state reflects events up to but not past target time.
   - `seek(duration + 100.0)` — clamps to duration.
   - `seek(-5.0)` — clamps to 0.
   - `seek_relative(+delta)` and `seek_relative(-delta)` — verify `current_time()` updates correctly.
   - Seek forward then backward — verify state is consistent (no accumulation artifacts from forward-only Vt).

3. **Tick tests:**
   - Start playing, `tick(small_dt)` — verify events are fed, `current_time()` advances, returns `true` if events were processed.
   - `tick(dt)` at 2× speed — verify `current_time()` advances by `2 * dt`.
   - `tick()` when paused — returns `false`, `current_time()` unchanged.
   - `tick()` that reaches end — auto-pauses (`is_playing() == false`), returns `true`. Subsequent `tick()` returns `false`.

4. **`time_to_next_event` tests:**
   - At start, playing at 1× — returns time to first event's effective time.
   - At 2× speed — returns half the time.
   - When paused or at end — returns `None`.

5. **Stepping tests:**
   - `step_forward()` from start while paused — advances to first output event, returns `true`.
   - `step_forward()` skips input/marker events (use `with_markers.cast`).
   - `step_forward()` at end — returns `false`.
   - `step_backward()` from mid-recording — goes to previous output event, verify `current_time()` reflects that event.
   - `step_backward()` at start — returns `false`.
   - Both step methods while playing — return `false`.

6. **Marker and insta snapshot tests:**
   - Load `with_markers.cast` — verify `markers()` returns 2 markers with correct labels and effective times (not raw).
   - Load `minimal_v2.cast`, seek to end, snapshot the screen text lines. Locks down the full pipeline: parse → time map → index → seek → render.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] All 6 test groups pass
- [ ] Load smoke test covers all valid files; specific tests for idle limit resolution and errors
- [ ] Seek accuracy verified with screen content checks
- [ ] Tick advancement and auto-pause verified
- [ ] Stepping verified for forward, backward, and boundary cases
- [ ] Markers have effective times
- [ ] At least one insta snapshot test
- [ ] `cargo test -p speedrun-core` all green
- [ ] `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` passes

title: Epic 2.2 — TerminalView Widget
description:
Implement the ratatui TerminalView widget that renders avt's terminal cell grid to the host terminal. This is a pure rendering component with no side effects — it maps avt cells to ratatui cells with correct colors, attributes, cursor rendering, and viewport scrolling.

## Context

The core engine (speedrun-core) provides `player.screen() -> &[avt::Line]` which gives access to the terminal cell grid. Each `avt::Line` contains `Cell(char, Pen)` tuples. The TerminalView widget must map these to ratatui buffer cells.

### Key design decisions

- **Cell-by-cell mapping** using `line.cells()` iterator which yields `(char, Pen)` pairs.
- **Color mapping** is direct: `avt::Color::Indexed(u8)` → `ratatui::Color::Indexed(u8)`, `avt::Color::RGB(RGB8)` → `ratatui::Color::Rgb(r, g, b)`, `None` → `Color::Reset`.
- **Attribute mapping**: `Pen` has `is_bold()`, `is_faint()`, `is_italic()`, `is_underline()`, `is_strikethrough()`, `is_blink()`, `is_inverse()` which map to ratatui `Modifier` flags.
- **Wide characters**: `char_width()` via `unicode-width` — wide chars occupy 2 cells, so the second cell must be skipped.
- **Cursor rendering**: if visible, apply `REVERSED` modifier at cursor position.
- **Viewport scrolling**: when host terminal is smaller than recording, the viewport follows the cursor.

### avt API surface (verified)

- `avt::Line::cells() -> impl Iterator<Item = (char, Pen)>` — cell-by-cell with character and style
- `avt::Cell(char, Pen)` — tuple struct, Cell is `pub(crate)` but accessed through `Line::cells()`
- `avt::Pen` — style information:
  - `foreground() -> Option<Color>`, `background() -> Option<Color>`
  - `is_bold()`, `is_faint()`, `is_italic()`, `is_underline()`, `is_strikethrough()`, `is_blink()`, `is_inverse()`
- `avt::Color` — `Indexed(u8) | RGB(rgb::RGB8)` where `RGB8 { r: u8, g: u8, b: u8 }`
- `avt::Vt::view() -> &[Line]` — the visible line grid
- `avt::Vt::cursor() -> Cursor` — `{ col: usize, row: usize, visible: bool }`

### ratatui API surface (verified)

- `Widget` trait: `fn render(self, area: Rect, buf: &mut Buffer)`
- `buf.get_mut(x, y) -> &mut Cell` — global coordinates (offset by area.x/area.y)
- `Cell::set_char(ch)`, `set_fg(Color)`, `set_bg(Color)`, `cell.modifier = Modifier::FLAGS`
- `Color::Indexed(u8)`, `Color::Rgb(u8, u8, u8)`, `Color::Reset`
- `Modifier::BOLD | DIM | ITALIC | UNDERLINED | SLOW_BLINK | REVERSED | HIDDEN | CROSSED_OUT`

## Scope

- `crates/speedrun/src/ui.rs` — new file

## Tickets

- 2.2.1 — Cell mapping and basic rendering
- 2.2.2 — Viewport and scroll offset
- 2.2.3 — Widget integration tests

title: 2.2.1 — Cell mapping and basic rendering
2.2.2 — Viewport and scroll offset
2.2.3 — Widget integration tests
notes:
## Test visibility for private helpers

The test code in this ticket calls `map_color()` and `map_modifiers()` directly. These are private functions in `ui.rs`. To make them testable, use one of these approaches:

**Recommended**: Put tests inside `ui.rs` as a `#[cfg(test)] mod tests {}` block. This gives tests access to private functions. The tests in this ticket should go inside `crates/speedrun/src/ui.rs`, not in a separate test file.

```rust
// At the bottom of ui.rs:
#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;
    use ratatui::buffer::Buffer;
    // ... all tests here have access to map_color, map_modifiers, etc.
}
```

**Alternative**: Make `map_color` and `map_modifiers` `pub(crate)` instead of private. This is acceptable since they're implementation details of the TUI crate, not part of a public library API.

## `rgb` crate import for tests

The color mapping tests use `rgb::RGB8 { r: 255, g: 128, b: 0 }`. The `rgb` crate must be available. It should already be in `Cargo.toml` (added in 2.2.1 or 2.1.1). Verify with:
```rust
use rgb::RGB8;
```

## Test data paths

Tests reference `../../testdata/minimal_v2.cast`. This path is relative to `crates/speedrun/src/` (two levels up from the source file to the workspace root, then into `testdata/`). However, `cargo test` runs from the crate root (`crates/speedrun/`), so the correct relative path is `../../testdata/minimal_v2.cast`. This matches how `speedrun-core` tests use the same pattern.

## `create_vt` is public

`speedrun_core::create_vt(cols, rows)` is re-exported from `lib.rs` and available for test usage. This is how the attribute mapping tests construct a Vt to feed SGR sequences to.

## avt dependency needed for tests

Tests import `avt::Color` for constructing test values (e.g., `avt::Color::Indexed(1)`). The `avt` dependency must be in `Cargo.toml` (should have been added in 2.2.1).

title: Epic 3.1+3.2 — Controls bar widget and behavior
description:
## Summary

Implement the controls bar: a single-row overlay showing playback state (icon, time, progress bar, speed), with responsive layout and auto-hide behavior. Combines spec epics 3.1 (widget) and 3.2 (behavior) since the overlay positioning and visibility logic are tightly coupled.

## Context

Phase 2 left `show_controls` and `last_interaction` fields on `App` but they are never read during rendering. The `--no-controls` flag is already parsed and passed to `App::new()`. No controls bar widget exists. This epic builds the widget from scratch and wires it into the render pipeline with full visibility state management.

## Dependency

This epic depends on the foundation ticket (3.0 — Action enum and input module) being merged to main first. The `ToggleControls` action variant will already exist; this epic wires its dispatch.

## Scope

- Controls bar ratatui Widget with all visual elements
- Time formatting helpers (M:SS, H:MM:SS)
- Progress bar calculation with marker tick positioning
- Responsive layout (progressive degradation at narrow widths)
- Overlay positioning (over bottom row vs below recording)
- Visibility state machine (auto-hide, show on interaction/pause/end, Tab toggle)
- `--no-controls` initial state integration
- `compute_timeout` updated for auto-hide timer

## Tickets

1. 3.1 — Controls bar widget (rendering, helpers, responsive layout)
2. 3.2 — Controls bar integration and auto-hide behavior

## Requirements covered

From REQUIREMENTS.md Epics 3.1 and 3.2 (lines 539-577).

title: 3.1 — Controls bar widget
description:
## Summary

Implement the controls bar as a ratatui `Widget` — a single-row display showing playback state icon, current/total time, a progress bar with marker ticks, and a speed indicator. Includes time formatting helpers, progress bar math, responsive layout, and distinct background styling.

## Context

No controls bar widget exists yet. `ui.rs` contains only `TerminalView` and `ViewportState`. The player API already exposes everything needed: `current_time()`, `duration()`, `speed()`, `is_playing()`, `markers()`, `size()`.

## Files

- `crates/speedrun/src/ui.rs` — add `ControlsBar` widget and helper functions (or a `controls.rs` submodule if preferred for organization, re-exported from `ui.rs`)

## Tasks

### 1. Time formatting helper

`fn format_time(seconds: f64) -> String`
- Under 1 hour: `M:SS` (e.g., `0:00`, `1:23`, `59:59`)
- 1 hour+: `H:MM:SS` (e.g., `1:00:00`, `12:34:56`)
- Negative values clamp to `0:00`
- NaN/infinity clamp to `0:00`

### 2. Progress bar calculation

`fn progress_bar_segments(width: u16, fraction: f64, marker_positions: &[f64]) -> (u16, u16, Vec<u16>)`
- Returns (filled_width, empty_width, marker_columns)
- `fraction` is `current_time / duration`, clamped to [0.0, 1.0]
- `filled_width = (width as f64 * fraction).round() as u16`
- `empty_width = width - filled_width`
- Marker columns: each marker's fractional position mapped to a column index within the bar width
- Zero width → (0, 0, vec![])

### 3. Controls bar widget struct

```rust
pub struct ControlsBar {
    is_playing: bool,
    is_at_end: bool,
    current_time: f64,
    duration: f64,
    speed: f64,
    marker_times: Vec<f64>,  // effective times of markers
}
```

The widget takes a snapshot of player state (not a reference to Player) so it's decoupled and testable.

### 4. Widget::render implementation

Layout the single row left-to-right:
1. **State icon** (2 chars): `▶ ` (playing), `▮▮` (paused), `■ ` (at end)
2. **Time display** (variable): `{current} / {total}` using `format_time`
3. **Gap** (1 char): space
4. **Progress bar** (flexible): `█` filled + `░` empty, with `│` marker ticks overlaid at marker positions
5. **Gap** (1 char): space
6. **Speed indicator** (variable): e.g., `1.0×`, `0.25×`

**Responsive degradation** when the row is too narrow:
- Progress bar shrinks first (has minimum useful width of ~10 chars)
- Below that: speed indicator is dropped
- Below that: duration (total time) is dropped, show only current time
- Below minimum viable (~8 chars for icon + time): hide controls entirely (render nothing)

**Background styling**: use a distinct background color (e.g., `Color::DarkGray` or similar) for the entire row to visually separate from recording content.

### 5. Snapshot tests

Create tests that render `ControlsBar` into a `Buffer` at various widths:
- **Wide** (80 cols): all elements visible, progress bar has room
- **Medium** (40 cols): progress bar shorter but all elements present
- **Narrow** (25 cols): speed dropped
- **Very narrow** (15 cols): duration dropped
- **Too small** (5 cols): nothing rendered

Use `insta::assert_snapshot!` for each.

### 6. Unit tests

- `format_time(0.0)` → `"0:00"`, `format_time(62.5)` → `"1:02"`, `format_time(3661.0)` → `"1:01:01"`
- `format_time(-1.0)` → `"0:00"`, `format_time(f64::NAN)` → `"0:00"`
- Progress bar at 0%, 50%, 100%
- Progress bar with markers (verify marker column positions)
- Progress bar at zero width → empty result

## Important: Widget trait

ratatui 0.26 `Widget` trait: `fn render(self, area: Rect, buf: &mut Buffer)`. The widget is consumed on render. Use `impl Widget for ControlsBar` (not a reference impl — keep it simple since the struct is cheap to construct).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `format_time` correctly formats seconds as `M:SS` or `H:MM:SS` with edge cases handled
- [ ] `ControlsBar` widget renders a single row with icon, time, progress bar, speed
- [ ] State icon shows `▶` when playing, `▮▮` when paused, `■` when at end
- [ ] Progress bar uses `█` for filled and `░` for empty segments
- [ ] Marker ticks (`│`) appear at correct positions on the progress bar
- [ ] Speed displays as `{speed}×` (e.g., `1.0×`, `0.25×`)
- [ ] Responsive layout degrades gracefully: bar shrinks → speed drops → duration drops → hide
- [ ] Background color distinguishes controls from recording content
- [ ] Snapshot tests cover wide, medium, narrow, very narrow, and too-small widths
- [ ] Unit tests cover time formatting and progress bar calculation edge cases
- [ ] `cargo build --workspace` passes with zero warnings
- [ ] `cargo test --workspace` passes
- [ ] `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` passes
- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes

title: 3.2 — Controls bar integration and auto-hide behavior
description:
## Summary

Wire the controls bar widget into the render pipeline with correct overlay positioning, and implement the full visibility state machine (auto-hide, show on interaction/pause/end, Tab toggle, --no-controls).

## Context

Ticket 3.1 created the `ControlsBar` widget but it is not rendered anywhere yet. `App` already has `show_controls: bool` and `last_interaction: Instant` fields. The `--no-controls` flag is parsed and passed as `!args.no_controls` to `App::new()` as the initial `show_controls` value. The `ToggleControls` action exists from the foundation ticket but is a no-op.

## Files

- `crates/speedrun/src/app.rs` — visibility state, render integration, timeout update, action dispatch
- `crates/speedrun/src/ui.rs` — overlay positioning logic (may be a helper function or part of render)

## Tasks

### 1. Overlay positioning logic

In `App::render()`, after rendering `TerminalView`, conditionally render `ControlsBar`:

- **Host terminal taller than recording:** render the controls bar in the row immediately below the recording content. No overlay — it sits in the extra space. Calculate `Rect` with `y = recording_rows`, `height = 1`, `width = min(recording_cols, area.width)`.
- **Host terminal same height or shorter:** render the controls bar over the bottom row of the visible area. Calculate `Rect` with `y = area.height - 1`, `height = 1`, `width = area.width`.
- Only render when `self.controls_visible()` returns true (see task 3).

Construct the `ControlsBar` from current player state (use whatever constructor pattern ticket 3.1 created — `::new()` or struct literal):
```rust
let is_at_end = !self.player.is_playing()
    && self.player.current_time() >= self.player.duration();
let controls = ControlsBar {
    is_playing: self.player.is_playing(),
    is_at_end,
    current_time: self.player.current_time(),
    duration: self.player.duration(),
    speed: self.player.speed(),
    marker_times: self.player.markers().iter().map(|m| m.time).collect(),
};
frame.render_widget(controls, controls_rect);
```

### 2. Visibility state machine

Replace the simple `show_controls: bool` with richer state. Add/modify fields on `App`:

```rust
/// Whether controls were manually hidden via Tab
controls_manually_hidden: bool,
/// Whether to force-show controls (overrides auto-hide)
controls_force_show: bool,
```

Keep `last_interaction: Instant` (already exists).

Implement `fn controls_visible(&self) -> bool`:
- If `controls_manually_hidden` → false
- If `controls_force_show` → true (covers: paused, at end, just interacted)
- If playing and `Instant::now() - last_interaction > Duration::from_secs(2)` → false (auto-hide)
- Otherwise → true

State transitions:
- **Any navigation keypress** (seek, step, speed change, marker jump, percentage jump): set `controls_force_show = true`, `controls_manually_hidden = false`, reset `last_interaction`. The auto-hide timer will clear `controls_force_show` after 2s.
- **Pause** (including auto-pause at end): `controls_force_show = true`, `controls_manually_hidden = false`
- **Play** (resume): `controls_force_show = false` (let auto-hide take over)
- **Tab**: toggle `controls_manually_hidden`. If toggling to hidden, clear `controls_force_show`.
- **Initial state**: if `--no-controls`, set `controls_manually_hidden = true`. Otherwise, `controls_force_show = true` (visible for initial 2s).

Note: the exact field design is flexible — the implementer may choose a simpler or different representation as long as the behavior matches. An enum-based state machine is also acceptable.

### 3. Update `handle_action` for controls-affecting actions

For actions already implemented (SeekForward, SeekBackward, TogglePlayback), add the visibility side-effects described above. Wire `ToggleControls` action:
```rust
Action::ToggleControls => {
    self.controls_manually_hidden = !self.controls_manually_hidden;
    if self.controls_manually_hidden {
        self.controls_force_show = false;
    }
}
```

### 4. Update `compute_timeout` for auto-hide

Currently returns `time_to_next_event().unwrap_or(100ms)`. When controls are visible during playback, the timeout must also account for the auto-hide deadline:

```rust
fn compute_timeout(&self) -> Duration {
    let playback = self.player.time_to_next_event()
        .unwrap_or(Duration::from_millis(100));

    if self.player.is_playing() && self.controls_visible() {
        let elapsed = self.last_interaction.elapsed();
        let hide_deadline = Duration::from_secs(2).saturating_sub(elapsed);
        playback.min(hide_deadline.max(Duration::from_millis(10)))
    } else {
        playback
    }
}
```

This ensures the event loop wakes up to hide controls even when no playback events are pending.

### 5. Trigger re-render on auto-hide

In the event loop, after the tick block, check whether controls visibility changed since last render. If so, set `needs_redraw = true`. A simple approach: track `last_controls_visible: bool` and compare each iteration.

### 6. Tests

**Important — testing time-dependent behavior:** The auto-hide logic uses `Instant::now()` which cannot be reliably tested with `thread::sleep`. Instead, manipulate `last_interaction` directly to simulate elapsed time:
```rust
// Simulate 3 seconds of no interaction
app.last_interaction = Instant::now() - Duration::from_secs(3);
assert!(!app.controls_visible());
```
This is the standard pattern — do NOT use `thread::sleep` in tests.

- **Visibility state machine unit tests** (construct App with `load_player("minimal_v2.cast")`, manipulate state, assert `controls_visible()`):
  - Initial state with `show_controls=true` → `controls_visible()` returns true
  - Initial state with `show_controls=false` (`--no-controls`) → `controls_visible()` returns false
  - Playing + `last_interaction` set to 3s ago → `controls_visible()` returns false (auto-hidden)
  - Playing + `last_interaction` set to 1s ago → `controls_visible()` returns true (within 2s window)
  - After calling `handle_action(SeekForward)` → `controls_visible()` returns true, `last_interaction` is recent
  - Paused → `controls_visible()` returns true regardless of `last_interaction`
  - Tab toggle → `controls_visible()` flips; manual hide persists even when `last_interaction` is recent
  - At end (seek to duration, player auto-pauses) → `controls_visible()` returns true
  - Tab to hide → `handle_action(SeekForward)` → `controls_visible()` returns true (manual hide cleared)

- **Overlay positioning unit tests** (test the Rect calculation logic, not full rendering):
  - Host 80x30, recording 80x24 → controls Rect at y=24 (below recording)
  - Host 80x24, recording 80x24 → controls Rect at y=23 (overlay bottom row)
  - Host 60x20, recording 80x24 → controls Rect at y=19 (overlay, scrolled view)

- **Render integration test** (use TestBackend):
  - Construct App, pause player (controls should be visible), render via `terminal.draw()`
  - Verify the bottom row of the buffer contains controls bar content (non-empty, has the state icon)

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Controls bar renders over the bottom row when host ≤ recording height
- [ ] Controls bar renders below recording when host > recording height
- [ ] Controls auto-hide after 2s of no user input during playback
- [ ] Controls show immediately on any navigation keypress (seek, step, speed, marker, percentage)
- [ ] Controls show on pause and remain visible while paused
- [ ] Controls show at end of recording (auto-pause)
- [ ] Tab toggles controls visibility; manual hide persists until next interaction or Tab
- [ ] `--no-controls` starts with controls hidden (only Tab or interaction shows them)
- [ ] `compute_timeout` wakes the event loop for auto-hide deadline
- [ ] Re-render triggers when controls visibility changes
- [ ] `cargo build --workspace` passes with zero warnings
- [ ] `cargo test --workspace` passes
- [ ] `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` passes
- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes
